query: How many credits do graduate students need to be full-time?

relevant_chunks.doc_name: Full-time Status < Illinois Institute of Technology
relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Full-time status requires registration in at least:
• Twelve (12) hours for undergraduate students or
• At least 9 hours for graduate students

Additional Notes:
• Graduate students are eligible for federal aid and continued deferment of existing loans if they are registered for half-time (4.5 hours) or greater. Graduate students taking less than half-time hours can file for a forbearance to extend their deferment.
• Continuing graduate students taking one or more hours of 591, 600, 691, or PSYC

Full-time degree-seeking students who wish to enroll for more than 18 credit hours or part-time degree-seeking students who wish to enroll in nine to eleven credit hours must obtain permission from their academic dean. Students who wish to enroll in more than two courses during the summ

Internal undergraduate transfer credit from Illinois Institute of Technology. 3. o Shared applicable unde

Doctoral degree candidates may transfer previously completed graduate work not applied toward a prior earned degree up to a maximum of 42 credit hours beyond the baccalaureate degree (which may include up to 32 credit hours from a completed master's degree) or 50 percent of their total Ph.D. program credit hour requirements, whichever is smaller. The work must be judged to be relevant to the current doctoral program, must have been completed with grades of 'B' or better, and must be acceptable f

Academic credit is recorded in credit hours. Each credit hour represents one 50-minute period per week for a 16-week semester. Illinois Tech follows the standard Carnegie Unit, requiring 750 contact minutes of instruction per credit hour, regardless of the length of the term. Non-degree continuing education programs award Continuing Education Units (CEU). In general, courses offered only for CEU credit use only Pass/Fail grading.

An official Co-Terminal Shared and Non-Shared Courses eForm is mandatory for co-terminal graduate degree programs and must be filed by a co-terminal student, for final approval, in the first semester of co-terminal enrollment and no later than the day after add/drop for the term. Shared credit courses must be completed with a grade of 'C' or better to be applied to the graduate degree requirements of a co-termi

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. The deadlines to add/drop and withdraw from courses are posted on the Academic Calendar . Please note that non-full term courses such as short courses have special deadlines (also noted on the Academic Calendar). Withdrawing from a Course: The action of withdrawing from a course is taken after the add/drop courses deadline. Dropping a Course: Dropping a course refers to having the course removed from the student's schedule before the add/drop deadline.

Withdrawal (“W”) Grade. The withdrawal grade is issued to students who withdraw from a class after the last day to add and drop classes. The “W” cannot be changed to a standard letter grade and does not fulfill the requirements of a graduate program.

Hardship Withdrawal. The Illinois Student Debt Assistance Act (the "Act") was signed into law on May 27, 2022. Section 20(a) of the Act requires that, beginning with the 2022-2023 school year, institutions of higher education have a policy instituting a physical or financial hardship withdrawal process. A student may apply for a hardship withdrawal from their academic enrollment at Illinois Tech due to an unforeseeable and documented circumstance that occurs after the 100% tuition refund deadline date (published on Illinois Tech's Academic Calendar ), which had a significant impact on the student's ability to continue in their course of study and be successful. Examples of a physical or financial hardship include: (i) serious injury or illness;

relevant_chunks.content_preview: Students will receive communication regarding the review outcome or any requests for further information within two weeks of submission. As a general rule, students are not eligible for hardship withdrawals if they have completed all course requirements (e.g., attended the final exam or submitted the final project/paper/portfolio). A student receiving federal and/or state financial assistance should contact the Office of Financial Aid to address any possible financial implications, including tho

gold_answer:
**Direct Answer:** Yes, there is a difference between withdrawing and dropping a course.

**Explanation:** Withdrawing from a course is taken after the add/drop deadline, and the student is responsible for any tuition and/or fees associated with the course. The withdrawn credits count toward the student's total maximum credit hours, and a letter grade of 'W' is assigned to the withdrawn course, which is visible on the transcript. In contrast, dropping a course refers to having the course removed from the student's schedule before the add/drop deadline, and the student will not be charged any tuition and/or fees associated with the course. The removed credits do not count toward the student's maximum credit hours, and the course will not show up on the student's transcript.

relevant_chunks.content_preview: To the extent that any work created by a student or students is other than for course credit or to satisfy a degree requirement and qualifies as 'personal research', as defined in Appendix K of the Illinois Tech Faculty Handbook, such work belongs to the student or students and any other individuals, including, but not limited to, Illinois Tech faculty members, who created it. The rights in any such work will be determined by applicable patent, copyright or other intellectual property rights sta

gold_answer: Yes, you can work on a research project with a professor and keep the intellectual property if the work is done for course credit or to satisfy a degree requirement. According to the Illinois Tech student handbook, such works belong exclusively to the students, as stated in the policies regarding copyright and patents. However, the actual rights will be determined by applicable patent, copyright, or other intellectual property rights statutes and the facts and circumstances of the particular collaborative effort. If the work is done outside of course credit, it may still belong to the student if it qualifies as 'personal research' as defined in Appendix K of the Illinois Tech Faculty Handbook.

Students who wish to audit a course need to register for the course and then submit the Course Audit Request Form to the Office of the Registrar during the current semester's registration period:

relevant_chunks.doc_name: Full-time Status < Illinois Institute of Technology
relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Full-time status requires registration in at least:
• Twelve (12) hours for undergraduate students or
• At least 9 hours for graduate students

Additional Notes:
• Graduate students are eligible for federal aid and continued deferment of existing loans if they are registered for half-time (4.5 hours) or greater. Graduate students taking less than half-time hours can file for a forbearance to extend their deferment.
• Continuing graduate students taking one or more hours of 591, 600, 691, or PSYC

query: How do I get an official transcript from IIT?

relevant_chunks.content_preview: Transcripts. An official transcript of a student's academic record at Illinois Institute of Technology is available only through the Office of the Registrar, upon the authorized request of the student. Illinois Tech has partnered with Parchment to take online requests for transcript orders. Law students should contact the Conviser Law Center Registrar's Office for services. All transcripts from 1980 to the present can be delivered in an electronic/PDF format. PDF transcripts viewed electronicall

Payment for orders must be received before a transcript request will be processed. $10 for each electronic/PDF transcript $12 for each printed transcript sent via the United States Postal Service first-class mail (regular mail) Federal Express (FedEx) delivery service is available for an additional fee Transcripts are packaged in a sealed security envelope. If the seal is broken, the transcript is no longer deemed official. Academic records for students pursuing doctoral-level credit prior to Au

How To Order. Click on the "Order Official Transcript" Tool. After placing your order, you will be contacted when your transcript request has been processed. If you do not have access to the internet, you may contact our service provider, Parchment, at 847.716.3005 for assistance with placing an order.

Can I Send an Attachment?. Yes! When placing your order, during the recipient selection process, you have the option to mark a checkbox noting that you will send an attachment. Follow all additional instructions and your attachment(s) will be included with your transcript.
Where Is My Order?. To check the status of your order, please click here and log in to your Parchment account. You will need your order or student ID number.

Can I Cancel My Order?. To cancel your order, please click here and log in to your Parchment account. You will need your order or student ID number. You may only cancel orders that have not been filled.

Illinois Student Debt Assistance Act. In accordance with law, the university may not: refuse to provide an unofficial transcript to a current or former student on the grounds that the student owes a debt condition the provision of an unofficial transcript on the payment of a debt, other than a fee charged to provide the transcript charge a higher fee for obtaining an unofficial transcript or provide less favorable treatment of a request for an unofficial transcript because a current or former st

Additional Assistance and Questions. If you have any questions regarding the online request system, please contact Parchment support and they will be able to answer any questions or assist you with ordering online.

Records Services. For more information on the records services provided by the Office of the Registrar, please select a link below. For graduation ceremony information, please visit the Commencement website . Law students should contact the Conviser Law Center Registrar's Office for services at REGQ@kentlaw.iit.edu . gold_answer:
To get an official transcript from IIT, you can follow these steps: 

Yes, you can get an official transcript from IIT. 
You can place an order through the "Order Official Transcript" Tool on the IIT website, or contact Parchment at 847.716.3005 for assistance. 
Payment for orders must be received before a transcript request will be processed, with a fee of $10 for each electronic/PDF transcript and $12 for each printed transcript sent via the United States Postal Service first-class mail. 
You can check the status of your order by logging in to your Parchment account, and contact the Office of the Registrar at 312-567-3100 or kguytonhamlin@illinoistech.edu for additional assistance.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. The deadlines to add/drop and withdraw from courses are posted on the Academic Calendar . Please note that non-full term courses such as short courses have special deadlines (also noted on the Academic Calendar). Withdrawing from a Course: The action of withdrawing from a course is taken after the add/drop courses deadline. Dropping a Course: Dropping a course refers to having the course removed from the student's schedule before the add/drop deadline.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: The student is responsible for any tuition and/or fees associated with the course. Dropping a Course: The student will not be charged any tuition and/or fees associated with the course.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: Withdrawn credits count toward the student's total maximum credit hours. Dropping a Course: The removed credits do not count toward the student's maximum credit hours.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: A letter grade of 'W' is assigned to the withdrawn course and is visible on the transcript. Dropping a Course: Since the course is entirely removed, the course will not show up on the student's transcript.

Doctoral degree candidates may transfer previously completed graduate work not applied toward a prior earned degree up to a maximum of 42 credit hours beyond the baccalaureate degree (which may include up to 32 credit hours from a completed master's degree) or 50 percent of their total Ph.D. program credit hour requirements, whichever is smaller. Academic credit is recorded in credit hours. Each credit hour represents one 50-minute period per week for a 16-week semester. Illinois Tech follows the standard Carnegie Unit, requiring 750 contact minutes of instruction per credit hour, regardless of the length of the term. Non-degree continuing education programs award Continuing Education Units (CEU). In general, courses offered only for CEU credit use only Pass/Fail grading.

Illinois Tech allows co-terminal students to share up to nine credit hours between their undergraduate and graduate programs. However, some co-terminal programs allow a smaller amount of shared credit or no shared credit. Shared courses should be included in the Co-Terminal Shared and Non-Shared Courses eForm, using the 'Shared (Used in both programs)' descriptor. gold_answer: Yes, dropping from 12 credits to 9 credits mid-semester will affect your tuition. According to the Institute of Design's tuition rates for Admitted Fall 2024 MDES students, the tuition for part-time students (fewer than 9 credits) is $1,888 per credit. This rate applies to students who are enrolled in fewer than 9 credits, which would be the case if you drop from 12 credits to 9 credits.

gold_answer: Yes. Dropping a course before the add/drop deadline may not result in repayment of financial aid, but withdrawing from a course after the deadline may require repayment of tuition and/or fees associated with the course, as stated in the Illinois Institute of Technology's financial aid policies. The Office of Financial Aid will update a student's FAFSA if they qualify for graduate federal aid after completing all undergraduate requirements.

relevant_chunks.content_preview: Registration Changes: Add/Drop, W Grades. Adding or dropping courses is done using the same procedure as initial registration. This is described on the How to Register page. Before the last day to add or drop, courses can be added or removed to a schedule. The changes made at this point will not be displayed on an official transcript, but will affect the total number of credit hours enrolled. This could affect the full- or part-time status of a student, and should be duly noted. Please refer to

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. The deadlines to add/drop and withdraw from courses are posted on the Academic Calendar . Please note that non-full term courses such as short courses have special deadlines (also noted on the Academic Calendar). Withdrawing from a Course: The action of withdrawing from a course is taken after the add/drop courses deadline. Dropping a Course: Dropping a course refers to having the course removed from the student's schedule before the add/drop deadline.

relevant_chunks.topic: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Request
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Request. A student may apply for a hardship withdrawal from their academic enrollment at Illinois Tech due to an unforeseeable and documented circumstance that occurs after the 100% tuition refund deadline date (published on Illinois Tech's Academic Calendar ), which had a significant impact on the student's ability to continue in their course of study and be successful. Examples of a physical or financial hardship include: (i) serious injury or illness;

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: The student is responsible for any tuition and/or fees associated with the course. Dropping a Course: The student will not be charged any tuition and/or fees associated with the course.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: Withdrawn credits count toward the student's total maximum credit hours. Dropping a Course: The removed credits do not count toward the student's maximum credit hours.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: A letter grade of 'W' is assigned to the withdrawn course and is visible on the transcript. Dropping a Course: Since the course is entirely removed, the course will not show up on the student's transcript.

relevant_chunks.doc_name: Full-time Status < Illinois Institute of Technology
relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Full-time status requires registration in at least:
• Twelve (12) hours for undergraduate students or
• At least 9 hours for graduate students

Additional Notes:
• Graduate students are eligible for federal aid and continued deferment of existing loans if they are registered for half-time (4.5 hours) or greater. Graduate students taking less than half-time hours can file for a forbearance to extend their deferment.
• Continuing graduate students taking one or more hours of 591, 600, 691, or PSYC

gold_answer:
**Direct Answer:** Yes, you will lose your financial aid and may be eligible for a tuition refund if you drop below full-time status mid-semester.

If you drop below full-time status (less than 12 hours for undergraduate students or less than 9 hours for graduate students) mid-semester, you will lose your financial aid. However, if you drop a course before the first two weeks of the semester, the course will not appear on your transcript and tuition will be refunded (Chunk 11). The tuition refund policy is not explicitly stated in the provided data, but it is implied that a refund may be available if you drop a course before the deadline.

relevant_chunks.topic: I. What is a co-terminal degree?
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Co-terminal degrees allow Illinois Tech students to simultaneously complete both an undergraduate and graduate degree (Bachelor's degree and Master's degree). Co-terminal students maintain their undergraduate student status while completing graduate coursework, and can maintain financial aid eligibility when applicable. Co-terminal degrees provide an opportunity for students to gain greater knowledge in specialized areas while completing a smaller number of credit hours with increased scheduling

Students applying to co-terminal studies must have completed at least 60 credit hours of undergraduate study and a least one full-time semester at Illinois Tech. Applicants must be at least one semester (12 credit hours) away from undergraduate graduation in order to apply. Applicants must also have a GPA of at least 3.0/4.0 or 3.25/4.0; however, please note that academic units may vary in terms of their GPA admission requirements. Co-terminal applications are coordinated through Illinois Tech's

Co-terminal students, like Illinois Tech undergraduate students, are required to complete their last 45 hours of undergraduate coursework at Illinois Tech. These hours may include approved co-terminal shared credit hours, but not purely graduate coursework.

Co-terminal students must maintain satisfactory grade point averages and a satisfactory rate of progress towards the completion of their co-terminal degrees. Illinois Tech considers co-terminal students to have an undergraduate student status throughout the course of their studies; however, students are subject to both undergraduate and graduate student probation policies. Co-terminal students who do not earn at least a 2.0 cumulative GPA, a 1.85 term GPA, a 2.0 major GPA, or who do not maintain

For master's degree programs, a maximum of nine applicable credit hours earned prior to matriculation into an Illinois Institute of Technology graduate degree program, subject to the graduate studies rules and restrictions, may be considered for: 1. External transfer credit for graduate transfer credit use. Applicable course credit hours may not have been applied toward any earned degree. 2. Internal undergraduate transfer credit from Illinois Institute of Technology. 3. o Shared applicable unde

Doctoral degree candidates may transfer previously completed graduate work not applied toward a prior earned degree up to a maximum of 42 credit hours beyond the baccalaureate degree (which may include up to 32 credit hours from a completed master's degree) or 50 percent of their total Ph.D. program credit hour requirements, whichever is smaller. The work must be judged to be relevant to the current doctoral program, must have been completed with grades of 'B' or better, and must be acceptable f

A number of formal co-terminal degree pairings exist, and can be viewed in the Undergraduate Bulletin. These legacy degree pairings have specific curricular and shared credit requirements. Students may alternatively work with advisers to identify customized bachelor's and master's degree pairings, pending the approval of the prospective graduate program and the student's undergraduate program. In these cases, shared credit is approved on an individual basis.

gold_answer: Yes, transfer credits can count toward degree requirements. For co-terminal students, up to nine graduate-level credit hours taken elsewhere and not applied toward any earned degree, with grades of 'B' or better, may be transferred towards the graduate portion of the co-terminal degree program, subject to the advance approval of the academic unit. Additionally, for master's degree programs, a maximum of nine applicable credit hours earned prior to matriculation may be considered for external transfer credit, and for doctoral degree candidates, up to 42 credit hours beyond the baccalaureate degree may be transferred.

gold_answer: Yes, transfer credits can affect GPA calculations. However, the specific GPA requirements for co-terminal students are outlined in the Coterminal Student Handbook, where it is stated that students must maintain a cumulative GPA of at least 2.0, a term GPA of at least 1.85, and a major GPA of at least 2.0.

relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Full-time status requires registration in at least:
• Twelve (12) hours for undergraduate students or
• At least 9 hours for graduate students

Additional Notes:
• Graduate students are eligible for federal aid and continued deferment of existing loans if they are registered for half-time (4.5 hours) or greater. Graduate students taking less than half-time hours can file for a forbearance to extend their deferment.
• Continuing graduate students taking one or more hours of 591, 600, 691, or PSYC

relevant_chunks.doc_name: Compressed Term Courses < Illinois Institute of Technology
relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Winter courses are a select group of courses within a compressed schedule that are part of either the fall or spring term. For example, when registering for these courses, if students select the spring term, only the winter courses offered in the spring will display. Winter courses are not part of a special term.

Academic credit is recorded in credit hours. Each credit hour represents one 50-minute period per week for a 16-week semester. Illinois Tech follows the standard Carnegie Unit, requiring 750 contact minutes of instruction per credit hour, regardless of the length of the term. Non-degree continuing education programs award Continuing Education Units (CEU). In general, courses offered only for CEU credit use only Pass/Fail grading.

gold_answer: Yes, if you take 9 credits as a graduate student, you are considered full-time. This is according to the Illinois Institute of Technology's policy, which states that full-time status for graduate students requires registration in at least 9 hours. This policy is consistent across various documents, including the Full-time Status document and the Registration Policies document.

query: If I take 12 credits as an undergraduate student, am I considered full-time?

relevant_chunks.doc_name: Full-time Status < Illinois Institute of Technology
relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Full-time status requires registration in at least:
• Twelve (12) hours for undergraduate students or
• At least 9 hours for graduate students

Additional Notes:
• Graduate students are eligible for federal aid and continued deferment of existing loans if they are registered for half-time (4.5 hours) or greater. Graduate students taking less than half-time hours can file for a forbearance to extend their deferment.
• Continuing graduate students taking one or more hours of 591, 600, 691, or PSYC

relevant_chunks.doc_name: Compressed Term Courses < Illinois Institute of Technology
relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Winter courses are a select group of courses within a compressed schedule that are part of either the fall or spring term. For example, when registering for these courses, if students select the spring term, only the winter courses offered in the spring will display. Winter courses are not part of a special term.
Winter courses are full-credit-hour courses, but it is important to note that they will not count towards a student's full-time status in any given term. For example, if an undergraduat

1. Undergraduate Students: The average full-time academic load during the fall or spring semester is 15 credit hours. The minimum registration required for full-time status for those semesters is 12 credit hours. Full-time degree-seeking students who wish to enroll for more than 18 credit hours or part-time degree-seeking students who wish to enroll in nine to eleven credit hours must obtain permission from their academic dean. Students who wish to enroll in more than two courses during the summ

For master's degree programs, a maximum of nine applicable credit hours earned prior to matriculation into an Illinois Institute of Technology graduate degree program, subject to the graduate studies rules and restrictions, may be considered for: 1. External transfer credit for graduate transfer credit use. Applicable course credit hours may not have been applied toward any earned degree. 2. Internal undergraduate transfer credit from Illinois Institute of Technology. 3. o Shared applicable unde

Doctoral degree candidates may transfer previously completed graduate work not applied toward a prior earned degree up to a maximum of 42 credit hours beyond the baccalaureate degree (which may include up to 32 credit hours from a completed master's degree) or 50 percent of their total Ph.D. program credit hour requirements, whichever is smaller. The work must be judged to be relevant to the current doctoral program, must have been completed with grades of 'B' or better, and must be acceptable f

Academic credit is recorded in credit hours. Each credit hour represents one 50-minute period per week for a 16-week semester. Illinois Tech follows the standard Carnegie Unit, requiring 750 contact minutes of instruction per credit hour, regardless of the length of the term. Non-degree continuing education programs award Continuing Education Units (CEU). In general, courses offered only for CEU credit use only Pass/Fail grading.

Illinois Tech allows co-terminal students to share up to nine credit hours between their undergraduate and graduate programs. However, some co-terminal programs allow a smaller amount of shared credit or no shared credit. Shared courses should be included in the Co-Terminal Shared and Non-Shared Courses eForm, using the 'Shared (Used in both programs)' descriptor. Shared credit courses must be completed with a grade of 'C' or better to be applied to the graduate degree requirements of a co-termi

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. The deadlines to add/drop and withdraw from courses are posted on the Academic Calendar . Please note that non-full term courses such as short courses have special deadlines (also noted on the Academic Calendar). Withdrawing from a Course: The action of withdrawing from a course is taken after the add/drop courses deadline. Dropping a Course: Dropping a course refers to having the course removed from the student's schedule before the add/drop deadline.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course

query: If I withdraw from a course, does it affect my GPA?

relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawal (“W”) Grade. The withdrawal grade is issued to students who withdraw from a class after the last day to add and drop classes. The “W” cannot be changed to a standard letter grade and does not fulfill the requirements of a graduate program.

Withdrawal from a Course. Non-attendance or expressing the intent to the instructor of a course does not constitute withdrawal from the course. Withdrawals are performed through the Illinois Tech Portal in the same way as adding or dropping a course. Students may withdraw from one or more courses by the published withdrawal deadline, which is approximately the 60 percent point of the term—see the Academic Calendar . Students who do not attend or participate in a course and fail to withdraw offic

Late Withdrawal. Withdrawals from courses after the published deadline are only permitted in extraordinary circumstances beyond a student's control, such as medical or personal emergencies, and must be accompanied with documentation related to the appeal. Appeals must be submitted after the withdrawal deadline noted in the Academic Calendar and before the final exam for the current term. Students are required to continue attending the course while the appeal is being reviewed. In the rare event

Hardship Withdrawal. The Illinois Student Debt Assistance Act (the "Act") was signed into law on May 27, 2022. Section 20(a) of the Act requires that, beginning with the 2022-2023 school year, institutions of higher education have a policy instituting a physical or financial hardship withdrawal process. According to the Act, the process must work to limit debt owed by students who have to withdraw from the institution due to a significant physical or financial hardship and to assist those studen

relevant_chunks.topic: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Policy
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Policy. Consistent with the Illinois Student Debt Assistance Act, this policy establishes processes and procedures to assist students in limiting their student debt when a physical or financial hardship requires the student to withdraw from Illinois Tech after the 100% tuition add/drop deadline has passed, as well as implementing requirements of the Act as it pertains to access to student transcripts. This policy should be read in conjunction with other

relevant_chunks.topic: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Request
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Request. A student may apply for a hardship withdrawal from their academic enrollment at Illinois Tech due to an unforeseeable and documented circumstance that occurs after the 100% tuition refund deadline date (published on Illinois Tech's Academic Calendar ), which had a significant impact on the student's ability to continue in their course of study and be successful. Examples of a physical or financial hardship include: (i) serious injury or illness;

relevant_chunks.topic: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Request
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Students will receive communication regarding the review outcome or any requests for further information within two weeks of submission. As a general rule, students are not eligible for hardship withdrawals if they have completed all course requirements (e.g., attended the final exam or submitted the final project/paper/portfolio). gold_answer: **Yes**, withdrawing from a course at Illinois Institute of Technology (IIT) can affect your GPA. The withdrawal grade ("W") is issued to students who withdraw from a class after the last day to add and drop classes, and it cannot be changed to a standard letter grade. This grade is worth 0 quality points, as indicated in the graduate academic policies.

Transcripts. **Direct Answer:** No, you do not get a tuition refund if you withdraw from all your classes in Spring 2026 after the 4th week. You will receive a "W" grade on your transcript. The tuition refund deadline for Spring 2026 is not specified in the provided data, but the withdrawal deadline for full semester courses is March 30, 2026.

**Explanation:** According to the Academic Calendar, the last day to withdraw for full semester courses is March 30, 2026. Since you are withdrawing after the 4th week, which is after the 60% point of the term, you are not eligible for a tuition refund. The "W" grade is issued to students who withdraw from a class after the last day to add and drop classes, which is consistent with your situation.

relevant_chunks.topic: I. What is a co-terminal degree?
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Co-terminal degrees allow Illinois Tech students to simultaneously complete both an undergraduate and graduate degree (Bachelor's degree and Master's degree). Co-terminal students maintain their undergraduate student status while completing graduate coursework, and can maintain financial aid eligibility when applicable. Co-terminal degrees provide an opportunity for students to gain greater knowledge in specialized areas while completing a smaller number of credit hours with increased scheduling

Illinois Tech considers co-terminal students to be degree-seeking, full-time, undergraduate students, and most Illinois Tech undergraduate scholarships will automatically be extended to the end of a co-terminal program. However, financial aid guidelines and regulations for coterminal students can differ from non-co-terminal students as aid may be applied differently throughout the course of the program based on student enrollment. Please consult the Office of Financial Aid website for the curren

A number of formal co-terminal degree pairings exist, and can be viewed in the Undergraduate Bulletin. These legacy degree pairings have specific curricular and shared credit requirements. Students may alternatively work with advisers to identify customized bachelor's and master's degree pairings, pending the approval of the prospective graduate program and the student's undergraduate program. In these cases, shared credit is approved on an individual basis.

relevant_chunks.topic: Co-Terminal Shared and Non-Shared Credit Form
relevant_chunks.content_preview: The Co-Terminal Shared and Non-Shared Courses eForm is the vehicle for establishing each co-terminal student's graduate degree plan with the Office of Graduate Academic Affairs. An official Co-Terminal Shared and Non-Shared Courses eForm is mandatory for co-terminal graduate degree programs and must be filed by a co-terminal student, for final approval, in the first semester of co-terminal enrollment and no later than the day after add/drop for the term. Failure to comply will result in the stud

relevant_chunks.topic: VIII. Transferring to a Different Co-Terminal Program
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Enrolled co-terminal students may transfer to another co-terminal program before the completion of their degree. Co-terminal students who wish to transfer to an equivalent degree or from a research to a professional degree within the same academic unit (M.S. to M.S., ME, or MAS; ME to ME; or MAS to MAS) may be required to complete a new co-terminal application. For certain degree programs a student may request the change by filing the Change of Degree, Major, and/or Declare Concentration, Projec

relevant_chunks.topic: IX. Withdrawing from the Co-Terminal Program
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Students may request to withdraw from the co-terminal program before the completion of the degree requirements. To place a withdrawal request, students may complete the Co-Terminal Withdrawal Form (G218) available at the Graduate Academic Affairs website (http://web.iit.edu/gaa/students/student-forms). Students who choose to withdraw from the co-terminal program will be converted to an undergraduate student status only and will be able to complete their undergraduate degree pursuant to Undergrad

1. Undergraduate Students: The average full-time academic load during the fall or spring semester is 15 credit hours. The minimum registration required for full-time status for those semesters is 12 credit hours. Full-time degree-seeking students who wish to enroll for more than 18 credit hours or part-time degree-seeking students who wish to enroll in nine to eleven credit hours must obtain permission from their academic dean. Students who wish to enroll in more than two courses during the summ

Students applying to co-terminal studies must have completed at least 60 credit hours of undergraduate study and a least one full-time semester at Illinois Tech. Applicants must be at least one semester (12 credit hours) away from undergraduate graduation in order to apply. Applicants must also have a GPA of at least 3.0/4.0 or 3.25/4.0; however, please note that academic units may vary in terms of their GPA admission requirements. Co-terminal applications are coordinated through Illinois Tech's

Co-terminal students are assigned a co-terminal adviser who will oversee academic advising and course selection for their graduate degree. Co-terminal students will continue to work with their major adviser on undergraduate degree concerns. Co-terminal students should work with their co-terminal adviser to develop their plan for graduate degree completion. All questions regarding graduate course selections, concentrations, specializations, or proposed project and thesis research should be direct

Students who wish to enroll in a co-terminal program should file a Declaration of Intent their first semester of enrollment at the university. This declaration signals an intent to apply to a coterminal program in the student's junior year, should academic criteria be met. This declaration is especially critical for students who wish to pursue a customized co-terminal degree pairing and will help identify any deficiencies or prerequisite courses for the prospective graduate degree program. The O

gold_answer:
**Direct Answer:** Yes, as a coterminal student, you are considered an undergraduate for full-time status purposes.

**Explanation:** According to the Coterminal Student Handbook, co-terminal students maintain their undergraduate student status while completing graduate coursework (Chunk 2). This status is also reflected in the Financial Aid section, which states that Illinois Tech considers co-terminal students to be degree-seeking, full-time, undergraduate students (Chunk 3). As a result, coterminal students are required to register for at least 12 credit hours to maintain full-time status, which is the same as undergraduate students (Chunk 9).

relevant_chunks.doc_name: Full-time Status < Illinois Institute of Technology
relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Full-time status requires registration in at least:
• Twelve (12) hours for undergraduate students or
• At least 9 hours for graduate students

Additional Notes:
• Graduate students are eligible for federal aid and continued deferment of existing loans if they are registered for half-time (4.5 hours) or greater. Graduate students taking less than half-time hours can file for a forbearance to extend their deferment.
• Continuing graduate students taking one or more hours of 591, 600, 691, or PSYC

query: Is there a fee for ordering an official transcript?

relevant_chunks.content_preview: Transcripts. An official transcript of a student's academic record at Illinois Institute of Technology is available only through the Office of the Registrar, upon the authorized request of the student. Illinois Tech has partnered with Parchment to take online requests for transcript orders. Law students should contact the Conviser Law Center Registrar's Office for services. All transcripts from 1980 to the present can be delivered in an electronic/PDF format. PDF transcripts viewed electronicall

Payment for orders must be received before a transcript request will be processed. $10 for each electronic/PDF transcript $12 for each printed transcript sent via the United States Postal Service first-class mail (regular mail) Federal Express (FedEx) delivery service is available for an additional fee Transcripts are packaged in a sealed security envelope. If the seal is broken, the transcript is no longer deemed official. Academic records for students pursuing doctoral-level credit prior to Au

How To Order. Click on the "Order Official Transcript" Tool. After placing your order, you will be contacted when your transcript request has been processed. If you do not have access to the internet, you may contact our service provider, Parchment, at 847.716.3005 for assistance with placing an order.

Can I Send an Attachment?. Yes! When placing your order, during the recipient selection process, you have the option to mark a checkbox noting that you will send an attachment. Follow all additional instructions and your attachment(s) will be included with your transcript.
Where Is My Order?. To check the status of your order, please click here and log in to your Parchment account. You will need your order or student ID number.

Can I Cancel My Order?. To cancel your order, please click here and log in to your Parchment account. You will need your order or student ID number. You may only cancel orders that have not been filled.

Illinois Student Debt Assistance Act. In accordance with law, the university may not: refuse to provide an unofficial transcript to a current or former student on the grounds that the student owes a debt condition the provision of an unofficial transcript on the payment of a debt, other than a fee charged to provide the transcript charge a higher fee for obtaining an unofficial transcript or provide less favorable treatment of a request for an unofficial transcript because a current or former st

Additional Assistance and Questions. If you have any questions regarding the online request system, please contact Parchment support and they will be able to answer any questions or assist you with ordering online.

Records Services. For more information on the records services provided by the Office of the Registrar, please select a link below. Academic credit is recorded in credit hours. Each credit hour represents one 50-minute period per week for a 16-week semester. Illinois Tech follows the standard Carnegie Unit, requiring 750 contact minutes of instruction per credit hour, regardless of the length of the term. Non-degree continuing education programs award Continuing Education Units (CEU). In general, courses offered only for CEU credit use only Pass/Fail grading.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. The deadlines to add/drop and withdraw from courses are posted on the Academic Calendar . Please note that non-full term courses such as short courses have special deadlines (also noted on the Academic Calendar). Withdrawing from a Course: The action of withdrawing from a course is taken after the add/drop courses deadline. Dropping a Course: Dropping a course refers to having the course removed from the student's schedule before the add/drop deadline.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course

According to the Illinois Institute of Technology's Academic Loads policy, students who drop a course may be affected by the change in their total credit hours enrolled, which could impact their full- or part-time status. However, if you drop a course before the add/drop deadline, you will not be charged any tuition and/or fees associated with the course, as stated in the Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course policy.

This is in accordance with the Illinois Institute of Technology's policy, as stated in the student handbook, which defines full-time status for undergraduate students as 12 credit hours or more. The tuition rates for full-time undergraduate students at the Institute of Design for the 2024-2025 academic year are $25,488 per term for the MDES program, $31,151 per term for the MDES + English Sequences program, and $28,902 per term for the MDES/Dual Degree (MBA) program.

relevant_chunks.topic: I. What is a co-terminal degree?
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Co-terminal degrees allow Illinois Tech students to simultaneously complete both an undergraduate and graduate degree (Bachelor's degree and Master's degree). Co-terminal students maintain their undergraduate student status while completing graduate coursework, and can maintain financial aid eligibility when applicable. Co-terminal degrees provide an opportunity for students to gain greater knowledge in specialized areas while completing a smaller number of credit hours with increased scheduling

Students applying to co-terminal studies must have completed at least 60 credit hours of undergraduate study and a least one full-time semester at Illinois Tech. Applicants must be at least one semester (12 credit hours) away from undergraduate graduation in order to apply. Applicants must also have a GPA of at least 3.0/4.0 or 3.25/4.0; however, please note that academic units may vary in terms of their GPA admission requirements. Co-terminal applications are coordinated through Illinois Tech's

All newly admitted co-terminal students who are eligible for federal financial aid are required to meet with a financial aid staff member to review the rules and regulations for financial aid as it pertains to the co-terminal program. Prior to their required meeting, students must meet with both undergraduate and co-terminal advisers to develop a projected course schedule to degree completion using the Co-Terminal Program Planning Worksheet .* This worksheet is essential for the Office of Financ

Co-terminal students must maintain satisfactory grade point averages and a satisfactory rate of progress towards the completion of their co-terminal degrees. Illinois Tech considers co-terminal students to have an undergraduate student status throughout the course of their studies; however, students are subject to both undergraduate and graduate student probation policies. This could affect the full- or part-time status of a student, and should be duly noted. Please refer to

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. The deadlines to add/drop and withdraw from courses are posted on the Academic Calendar . Please note that non-full term courses such as short courses have special deadlines (also noted on the Academic Calendar). Withdrawing from a Course: The action of withdrawing from a course is taken after the add/drop courses deadline. Dropping a Course: Dropping a course refers to having the course removed from the student's schedule before the add/drop deadline.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: The student is responsible for any tuition and/or fees associated with the course. Dropping a Course: The student will not be charged any tuition and/or fees associated with the course.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: Withdrawn credits count toward the student's total maximum credit hours. Dropping a Course: The removed credits do not count toward the student's maximum credit hours.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: A letter grade of 'W' is assigned to the withdrawn course and is visible on the transcript. Dropping a Course: Since the course is entirely removed, the course will not show up on the student's transcript.

gold_answer: **Yes**, your financial aid may be affected if you drop below full-time status. According to the Illinois Institute of Technology's policies, full-time status requires registration in at least 12 hours for undergraduate students or 9 hours for graduate students. If you drop below full-time status, your tuition and/or fees associated with the course may not be charged, but you will still be responsible for any tuition and/or fees associated with the course if you withdraw from it. Additionally, your financial aid package may be adjusted based on your enrollment status.

relevant_chunks.topic: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Policy
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Policy. Consistent with the Illinois Student Debt Assistance Act, this policy establishes processes and procedures to assist students in limiting their student debt when a physical or financial hardship requires the student to withdraw from Illinois Tech after the 100% tuition add/drop deadline has passed, as well as implementing requirements of the Act as it pertains to access to student transcripts. This policy should be read in conjunction with other

relevant_chunks.topic: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Request
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Request. A student may apply for a hardship withdrawal from their academic enrollment at Illinois Tech due to an unforeseeable and documented circumstance that occurs after the 100% tuition refund deadline date (published on Illinois Tech's Academic Calendar ), which had a significant impact on the student's ability to continue in their course of study and be successful. Examples of a physical or financial hardship include: (i) serious injury or illness;

relevant_chunks.topic: Physical or Financial Hardship Withdrawal Request
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Students will receive communication regarding the review outcome or any requests for further information within two weeks of submission. As a general rule, students are not eligible for hardship withdrawals if they have completed all course requirements (e.g., attended the final exam or submitted the final project/paper/portfolio). A student receiving federal and/or state financial assistance should contact the Office of Financial Aid to address any possible financial implications, including tho

Withdrawal (“W”) Grade. The withdrawal grade is issued to students who withdraw from a class after the last day to add and drop classes. The “W” cannot be changed to a standard letter grade and does not fulfill the requirements of a graduate program.

Withdrawal from a Course. Non-attendance or expressing the intent to the instructor of a course does not constitute withdrawal from the course. Withdrawals are performed through the Illinois Tech Portal in the same way as adding or dropping a course. Students may withdraw from one or more courses by the published withdrawal deadline, which is approximately the 60 percent point of the term—see the Academic Calendar . Students who do not attend or participate in a course and fail to withdraw offic

Late Withdrawal. Withdrawals from courses after the published deadline are only permitted in extraordinary circumstances beyond a student's control, such as medical or personal emergencies, and must be accompanied with documentation related to the appeal. Appeals must be submitted after the withdrawal deadline noted in the Academic Calendar and before the final exam for the current term. Students are required to continue attending the course while the appeal is being reviewed. In the rare event

Hardship Withdrawal. The Illinois Student Debt Assistance Act (the "Act") was signed into law on May 27, 2022. Section 20(a) of the Act requires that, beginning with the 2022-2023 school year, institutions of higher education have a policy instituting a physical or financial hardship withdrawal process. relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. The deadlines to add/drop and withdraw from courses are posted on the Academic Calendar . Please note that non-full term courses such as short courses have special deadlines (also noted on the Academic Calendar). Withdrawing from a Course: The action of withdrawing from a course is taken after the add/drop courses deadline. Dropping a Course: Dropping a course refers to having the course removed from the student's schedule before the add/drop deadline.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: The student is responsible for any tuition and/or fees associated with the course. Dropping a Course: The student will not be charged any tuition and/or fees associated with the course.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: Withdrawn credits count toward the student's total maximum credit hours. Dropping a Course: The removed credits do not count toward the student's maximum credit hours.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: A letter grade of 'W' is assigned to the withdrawn course and is visible on the transcript. Dropping a Course: Since the course is entirely removed, the course will not show up on the student's transcript.

**Direct Answer:** Yes, withdrawing from all courses mid-semester may affect your financial aid.

**Explanation:** If you withdraw from all courses mid-semester, you may be responsible for any tuition and/or fees associated with the courses, as per the Illinois Tech policy (Chunk 10). Additionally, withdrawn credits count toward your total maximum credit hours (Chunk 11). You should contact the Office of Financial Aid to address any possible financial implications, including those related to federal and/or state financial assistance (Chunk 3).

gold_answer: **Yes**, dropping from full-time to part-time status may change your grants or loans. Graduate students taking less than half-time hours (4.5 hours) can file for a forbearance to extend their deferment, but this may affect their federal aid and loan deferment. The tuition rates for part-time students are $1,835 per credit hour, and they may also be eligible for a CTA U-Pass for $146 per semester (or $79 for the summer term) if they opt in.

relevant_chunks.topic: I. What is a co-terminal degree?
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Co-terminal degrees allow Illinois Tech students to simultaneously complete both an undergraduate and graduate degree (Bachelor's degree and Master's degree). Co-terminal students maintain their undergraduate student status while completing graduate coursework, and can maintain financial aid eligibility when applicable. Co-terminal degrees provide an opportunity for students to gain greater knowledge in specialized areas while completing a smaller number of credit hours with increased scheduling

Students applying to co-terminal studies must have completed at least 60 credit hours of undergraduate study and a least one full-time semester at Illinois Tech. Applicants must be at least one semester (12 credit hours) away from undergraduate graduation in order to apply. Applicants must also have a GPA of at least 3.0/4.0 or 3.25/4.0; however, please note that academic units may vary in terms of their GPA admission requirements. Co-terminal applications are coordinated through Illinois Tech's

Co-terminal students, like Illinois Tech undergraduate students, are required to complete their last 45 hours of undergraduate coursework at Illinois Tech. These hours may include approved co-terminal shared credit hours, but not purely graduate coursework.

Co-terminal students must maintain satisfactory grade point averages and a satisfactory rate of progress towards the completion of their co-terminal degrees. Illinois Tech considers co-terminal students to have an undergraduate student status throughout the course of their studies; however, students are subject to both undergraduate and graduate student probation policies. Co-terminal students who do not earn at least a 2.0 cumulative GPA, a 1.85 term GPA, a 2.0 major GPA, or who do not maintain

For master's degree programs, a maximum of nine applicable credit hours earned prior to matriculation into an Illinois Institute of Technology graduate degree program, subject to the graduate studies rules and restrictions, may be considered for: 1. External transfer credit for graduate transfer credit use. Applicable course credit hours may not have been applied toward any earned degree. 2. Internal undergraduate transfer credit from Illinois Institute of Technology. 3. o Shared applicable unde

A number of formal co-terminal degree pairings exist, and can be viewed in the Undergraduate Bulletin. These legacy degree pairings have specific curricular and shared credit requirements. Students may alternatively work with advisers to identify customized bachelor's and master's degree pairings, pending the approval of the prospective graduate program and the student's undergraduate program. In these cases, shared credit is approved on an individual basis.

gold_answer: Yes, graduate students can transfer credits into a master's program at Illinois Institute of Technology. A maximum of nine applicable credit hours earned prior to matriculation into a graduate degree program may be considered for external transfer credit for graduate transfer credit use, subject to the graduate studies rules and restrictions. This credit must have been earned with grades of 'B' or better and not applied toward any earned degree.

relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: A letter grade of 'W' is assigned to the withdrawn course and is visible on the transcript. Dropping a Course: Since the course is entirely removed, the course will not show up on the student's transcript.

Transcripts. An official transcript of a student's academic record at Illinois Institute of Technology is available only through the Office of the Registrar, upon the authorized request of the student. Illinois Tech has partnered with Parchment to take online requests for transcript orders. Law students should contact the Conviser Law Center Registrar's Office for services. All transcripts from 1980 to the present can be delivered in an electronic/PDF format. PDF transcripts viewed electronicall

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: The student is responsible for any tuition and/or fees associated with the course. Dropping a Course: The student will not be charged any tuition and/or fees associated with the course.

relevant_chunks.topic: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course
relevant_chunks.content_preview: Withdrawing from vs. Dropping a Course. Withdrawing from a Course: Withdrawn credits count toward the student's total maximum credit hours. Dropping a Course: The removed credits do not count toward the student's maximum credit hours.

Payment for orders must be received before a transcript request will be processed. $10 for each electronic/PDF transcript $12 for each printed transcript sent via the United States Postal Service first-class mail (regular mail) Federal Express (FedEx) delivery service is available for an additional fee Transcripts are packaged in a sealed security envelope. If the seal is broken, the transcript is no longer deemed official. Academic records for students pursuing doctoral-level credit prior to Au

Changes in a student's schedule of classes, including a transfer from one course section to another, must be made no later than the relevant semester's specified Add/Drop deadline. Please consult the Academic Calendar for exact deadlines. A course dropped during the first two weeks of the semester will not appear on a student's transcript and tuition will be refunded. Failure to drop a course formally will result in a grade of 'NA' (apparent withdrawal due to non-attendance) for that course. A s

How To Order. Click on the "Order Official Transcript" Tool. After placing your order, you will be contacted when your transcript request has been processed. If you do not have access to the internet, you may contact our service provider, Parchment, at 847.716.3005 for assistance with placing an order.

Before registration, all undergraduate students must receive approval from their faculty adviser. All add/drop requests, except for change of section, also require adviser approval. The faculty adviser will provide the student with a PIN (also referred to as an alternate PIN), which will be used in registering for courses in the Illinois Tech portal.

Graduate students are encouraged to consult with a faculty adviser prior to registration. All graduate students registering for research courses n

relevant_chunks.doc_name: Late Registration Request < Illinois Institute of Technology
relevant_chunks.content_preview:
Illinois Institute of Technology expects all students to finalize their registration of courses in accordance with university deadlines. In extreme circumstances, a late registration request can be submitted for review to the student's academic advisor, course instructor, and, if applicable, department chair.

Students who wish to request late registration must complete the Late Registration Request, including all pertinent information, found on the Registrar - Student Resources page of the Illi

Can I Send an Attachment?. Yes! When placing your order, during the recipient selection process, you have the option to mark a checkbox noting that you will send an attachment. Follow all additional instructions and your attachment(s) will be included with your transcript.

gold_answer: Yes, if you drop a course after the add/drop deadline, it will appear on your transcript with a grade of 'W' (withdrawn). This is because dropping a course after the deadline is considered withdrawing from the course, which results in a 'W' grade being assigned and visible on the transcript. The deadlines to add/drop and withdraw from courses are posted on the Academic Calendar, and the specific dates for Spring 2026 are January 20 for full semester and online A session courses, January 27 for ID full semester and ID A session courses, March 17 for online B session courses, and March 30 for ID B session courses.
